comments.title: Past lives?
comments.content:
I have a question about possible past lives. When I was about 11 or 12 years old, I had two unusual dreams. In one, I briefly saw myself as a conquistador about to board a Spanish ship for the New World. That’s it, a single scene. Next, I had a dream about being a man named Bob who died in 1984. Again, it was a single scene of Bob’s funeral. In both dreams, I knew without a doubt that it was me.

When I was 13 years old, one day I was watching as two girls in my class were dancing to some music video. I had a sudden vision that seemed to merge with the scene that was currently unfolding before my eyes: one of the girls (whom I had a crush on) suddenly looked like a woman that I knew had been Bob’s wife. It was like a scene from Bob’s life had suddenly come to the present and merged with this girl dancing. It felt so very real.

Recently, I read a short book on the Spanish conquest of Mexico. The book recounted the final battle at which Tenochtitlan fell. While reading, it was like my mind was transported to that battle and I saw it unfolding before my eyes. I felt the terror the conquistadors experienced, especially the fear of capture and possibly being sacrificed by the Aztec if the battle failed. I don’t normally have such vivid experiences while reading.

There were other accounts in the book, closely related to the conquistadors present with Cortés. They produced similar, vivid “recollections.” This did not happen for accounts where Cortés was not present or nearby. It felt as though I had been present at the conquest, mostly at the side of Cortés himself. I think it was as a “loyalist” to him.

On a related matter, I’ve also never been able to loathe or detest Cortés, despite not knowing much about him until fairly recently. This, despite the often obligatory “all Europeans bad, all Natives good” history lessons I received in school.

The above is kind of long but I hope the background will help to answer my questions:

1. Could these be genuine past life memories? As a guy named Bob and a conquistador intensely loyal to his commander?

2. These first happened as I hit puberty. Does that have an effect on this kind of thing? I’ve heard of small children who recall details of past lives but I also know that awakening sexual energies have occult aspects.

3. Would meditating on all this potentially help me to recall more details or something else of importance?

4. The bulk of these experiences seem to be recollections of a life as a Spanish conquistador. Might there be a specific reason why I seem to recall so much about a single lifetime five hundred years ago?

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
1) Those could be past life memories, yes. They could also come from other sources. The best thing to do, in my experience, is to write down as much as you remember, and then see what else surfaces. When you're ready to remember more details, they'll come.

2) That happens sometimes, yes.

3) Rather than meditating on them, simply remember as much as you can, and then see whether other glimpses come up. If you're ready, they will.

4) Yes, but it's by no means certain you'll find out what it is. Karma is complex, and there are other factors as well. I have no idea, for example, why I consistently remember recipes from one of my past lives; yes, I was a housewife in that life and a good cook, but there was more going on in her life than that...

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
On the subject of possibly having once lived as a man named Bob: I was born in 1986, so only about two years would have passed between my life as Bob and this one. From the literature that I've read, the time between lifetimes can be hundreds of years.

1. Would such a short period of time (in this world, at least) possibly count against the possibility that the "Bob memories" are genuine past life recollections?

On the subject of possibly having been a conquistador: even when I was a monotheist, I didn't possess any ill will or negative feelings about the gods of most pantheons, with the exception of the Aztec. I intensely disliked them, and the recent decision in California has only reminded me of how much I really don't like these particular beings.

2. In addition to writing down potential memories, would also writing down tidbits like that also help? Cicada Grove mentioned recurring themes, and as I think about that, I think I might already be spotting some that relate to this potential past life.

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
1) These days, with so many human bodies constantly being born, it's quite common for the gap between lives to be very short. I'm fairly sure that's what's behind the transgender phenomenon, among other things -- people spent so little time out of incarnation that they can remember what their previous body used to feel like, and since they don't have a knowledge of reincarnation to explain it, it's a problem for them.

2) That's a useful thing to do, yes.

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
Some additional questions:

1. If karma is a universal force or mechanism, is this earthly life the only place where bad karma is worked out? For example, as a Catholic who hopes for eternity with Christ, perhaps any karma that I possess might also be dealt with in purgatory? Traditionally, purgatory has been for the purification and punishment of venial sins and temporal punishments due in this life. But it occurred to me that could just as easily be describing the accumulated karma of multiple lifetimes, too.

2. A related question comes from this speculation. In Catholicism, we have a concept of redemptive suffering, so that if we accept our suffering in faith and offer it up to the Holy Trinity, it functions as a form of purgatory in this life. If someone, regardless of faith tradition, is aware of their bad karma, could they do something similar? And perhaps have it be more effective than blindly or unknowingly working out their karma?

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
1) The Catholic doctrine of purgatory is based on reality. Between lives, you process the experience of the life you've just finished, and that takes care of some of the karmic burden through the simple effect of becoming aware of your life. Still, the church is right that it'll only clear away minor issues by itself. Think of incarnation as the school year and the time between lives as summer vacation; you can get some benefit from looking back over the school year and trying to figure out why you got a D in math, but the real learning comes when you go back to school in the fall and work harder at it.

2) That's another place where your church is quite correct. If you accept your karmic burden, make an effort to understand what it's trying to teach you, and ask for help from the beings you worship in order to make the best use of it, you will clear it away much more readily than if you whine about it, insist that it's all everyone else's fault, or what have you.

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content: I've read that the Toltec ruler Topiltzin, who was later mythologically identified with Quetzalcoatl, tried to ban the practice of human sacrifice, and replace it with sacrifices of animals, butterflies, flowers, and one's own blood. He was deposed, possibly by tribal or religious conflict, and this may have been mythologized as Quetzalcoatl departing from earthly affairs of consequence. He was rarely invoked in public ceremonies, and continued mostly as a patron of arts and merchants. The practice of mass human sacrifice was resumed, but the Aztec nobility continued to claim legitimacy by descent from Quetzalcoatl, even as they escalated what Topiltzin had opposed. I've also read, though I don't know how much to believe it, that by the time of the Spanish conquest, Quetzalcoatl's followers practiced human sacrifice much more sparingly, or even that his priests were against it. Maybe #NotAllAztecDeities?... Or maybe more like, #NotAllAztecDeitiesWellIMeanNotAsMuchAsTheOthersAnyway.

replies.title: Re: Past lives?
replies.content:
The myths surrounding Quetzalcoatl are very interesting. In some of them, he practices human sacrifice and in others, he condemns human sacrifice. The ruler known as Topiltzin, and the possible link to Quetzalcoatl, is also very fascinating. I haven't had a chance to study either in greater depth, but I can certainly agree that not all Aztec Deities were on the same level when it came to things like human sacrifice. I'm sure, for example, that Aztec commoners worshiped some kind of household gods or spirits, and that this worship probably didn't involve human sacrifice.

I mostly mention this aversion to the Aztec pantheon because it is instinctive, or visceral. I don't normally have those kinds of reactions to foreign cultures or pantheons. I suspect it might be a theme or a bit of data coming from a past life. If I was a Spanish conquistador, and witnessed firsthand the horrors of human sacrifice as part of my service, then that is perhaps the source of this gut reaction. I've begun journaling on this, and already I've noticed additional themes and "vague recollections" that seem consistent with this possibility.

I don't know if this is relevant, but the word "Aya" in Persian is a "question word"-putting it at the beginning of a declarative sentence makes the sentence a yes/no question, similar to the role "do", "will", and "are" play in many English questions.

(For example, "Aya emshab be-khaane-ye-man mee-aa'eed?"*, "Are you coming to my house tonight?" Take the "Aya" away and that sentence is just "You are coming to my house tonight"-a statement.)

*If anyone wants to try to sound this out, the two aa's together make a sound like the "a" in "father", or the sound you probably make when a doctor asks you to say "ahhh".

comments.title: etheric exercise
comments.content:
Hi JMG, here's a quick note about the etheric exercise you posted a few weeks ago, with a small question at the end. Of Judson's two books which you linked in that post, I've read the shorter of the two, and have just begun looking at the longer one.

I've done some experiments both with the original method (as Judson describes it in the shorter book) and with your modified version. Both seem to have a positive effect, but Judson's version seems to work much better for me. I can point to three differences in my experience of the results:
(a) It feels far more potent in the moment, in the simple sense that "something's moving," energetically-speaking, in the act of performing it.
(b) As soon as I finish, there's a greater sense both of etheric cleanliness, and of improved general vitality.
(c) That sense of cleanliness and vitality persists longer, after the exercise.

A few other notes:

(1) Vividly *imagining* the process, while seated in a chair, is at least as potent as actually standing up and moving my physical body, if not even moreso. I'm not sure how much this has to do with some feature of the exercise itself, and how much it's a result of being less self-conscious (i.e., not worrying about literally tripping over my own feet and stumbling while spinning in circles), such that I can focus my attention in more useful ways on clearing and moving the currents.

(2) During the spinning motions, the grasping motion of the arms (described by Judson, omitted in your version) seems important. I have no idea *why* a motion of "grasping toward" myself would help with *clearing* energies, but it certainly seems to.

(3) During both of the grasping motions, it seems most natural to have my arms pointed at about 45°; i.e., midway between out-to-the-side and directly forward. The two arms then form a right angle which is bisected by my line of vision straight ahead.

(4) It seems especially potent around sundown, give-or-take; moreso than in mid-afternoon. That's consistent with Judson's suggestions for timing. (She mentions both early in the morning and at the end of the day.)

I'll continue to experiment.

Finally, the small question: Is my observation, that this works as well (or better) in the imagination, consistent with how etheric workings are generally understood on first principles? I ask neither to support nor invalidate the data point, but rather to get a sense of how much it might be worth generalizing from this experience to other etheric phenomena. I don't have as good of a grip on "the etheric" as I might like, and I hope to improve on that.

Also, I'm curious: for anyone else who may be trying this, how does what I've described here fit (or not) with your experiences?

comments.title: Sol-Saturn transit
comments.content:
I. Suppose the Sun is applying to conjoin your natal Saturn, within one degree of arc. What sort of activities might be best favored and most liable to be fruitful during such a transit?

II. I find the current socio-political situation to be quite frightening. In particular, the possibility that a "vaccine passport" may become a standard practice strikes me as the worst idea ever proposed in my lifetime and, if implemented, likely to be the end of liberty as we have known it. In my Lilliputian way, I would like to do my tiny magical best to thwart it.

In your view, what model of practice would be most effective to this end? That detailed by M. Furth in "The Sorcerous Struggle for Albion," or perchance that of the Disorder Wizards on the Interweb - or maybe the Etheric methods of T. Hieronymous Bosch? I'm being deliberately a little coy here so that the opposition are less likely to successfully, er, Fortify the Election, if you take my meaning.

Also, what is the best mental attitude with which to approach such a project? Assume basic HOGD competence on my part, and - I think - a good relationship with Helios.

replies.title: Re: Sol-Saturn transit
replies.content:
1) It would depend on the rest of your natal chart, of course. What house your natal Saturn is in, what house or houses it rules, what other planets are in those houses, and what planets aspect Saturn or those other planets, are all important factors; so is the natal placement of the Sun by sign and house, and its aspects with other planets in your natal chart.

2) The basic rule for successful practice in such a case is always, always, ALWAYS to build up what you want rather than trying to oppose what you don't want. The first of your options is a solid and effective way of doing that, and it has the advantage that it's almost impossible for opponents to mess with it. Furth's opponents, the Zanies, certainly gave it the old bierkeller try, you know, and their efforts flopped. "Let It Be Energized Relative To You" is a wholly suitable focus for your work, and one that can be used in exactly the same way as Furth did with certain other images and concepts; given basic HOGD competence, you have a wider range of technical methods available than Furth did -- just for starters, I'll take the risk of murmuring the phrase "flashing tablet" in your hearing.

Great pandemonium accompanied and followed this exhibition. We then watched the rest of the movie. For me the whole experience was of a piece.

replies.title: Om Mani Padme Hum Mantra
replies.content:
This is the most widely used Tibetan Buddhist mantra. It’s used by lots of people in Tibet who haven’t been trained in the details of Vajrayana practice but have confidence that chanting the mantra will bless their minds. It is also carved into the rocks and written on prayer flags.

I did a quick search online to find possible useful resources for you:

https://blog.buddhagroove.com/om-mani-padme-hum/

https://www.rigpawiki.org/index.php?title=Mani_mantra

https://www.shambhala.com/snowlion_articles/om-mani-padme-hum-dalai-lama/

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1MtGVA9upDw The mantra, voices only

If you’re thinking of adding this mantra to your usual practices, you might say the mantra for five minutes considering that you are invoking Avalokiteshvara, the Buddha of compassion. (Do a google search for images and find one that appeals to you, that “feels right.”) Tibetan Buddhism invokes many different Buddhas and bodhisattvas but in essence they are all of the same nature.

So you start with the intention that you are practicing for yourself and also for others who need support. You say the mantra, or chant it. After a while, if you wish you visualize rays of light from this Buddha blessing your heart and mind. You repeat the mantra so your wisdom and compassion increase. I would also pray, may my discerning wisdom increase, may I make appropriate choices. You could also pray, may this practice enhance the other practices I am doing.

You do the practice with a light touch. No need to grade yourself.

After a period of practice, you close the practice with the wish that this practice may benefit other sentient beings.

It’s also fine to do the practice informally, just chant the mantra while you do the dishes and sweep the floor.

So you practice in this way and your energy centers will be just fine. No need to think about particular energy centers. It’s spring; we’re planting seeds; we know they will grow. This is a tool that has been used by millions of people including many great masters. Have relaxed confidence.

Just my two cents!!

EllenZ

replies.title: Om mani padme hung

This mantra is intimately linked to Avalokitesvara (Sanskrit) or Chenrezig (Tibetan).

Each syllable/word is linked to a certain visualization done with Chenrezig above you and facing you, projecting colored rays of lights into different parts of your body. So your hunch is right.

Almost anybody who visits is encouraged to say the mantra, though, though there are different "grades" of the visualization, some of which I believe should only be passed on by a qualified teacher. I can't remember the differences now, as it's been too long.

Above you and facing you if you feel drawn to the practice, and get good results from it. And start looking for a qualified Lama either in person or online to learn the details!

Hope that's helpful!

replies.title: parents
replies.content:
Gay sex columnist Dan Savage tells his readers that their presence is their only weapon. This is usually advice for gay readers who have been given a hard time after coming out as homosexual to rejecting parents. He tells them to draw the boundaries clearly and refuse to call or visit, etc. if not treated respect. Draw the line and stick to it.

Now a less confrontational approach is based on reinforcement theory. Basically, ignore or walk away from negative treatment and give positive treatment to any positive actions. The example one author gave was calls to her mother. She was tired of hearing "you never call, blah blah" So when her mom would talk in that vein she would just give noncommittal humms, and uhs. As soon as mom said anything neutral or interesting, like "Its raining here" or "your sister is going to Chicago next week" the writer would show interest in a positive tone of voice: "oh really, that sounds like fun" or "we haven't had rain yet". If mom turns negative again "why Chicago, its dirty and dangerous" it's back to "hmmm, etc." but no argument. She said that in a few episodes of this she was having mostly pleasant conversations with her mom. Reinforcement theory got a lot of impetus from training animals such as dolphins that can just withdraw from punishments, but will work for fish.

Abbott, who was taught this magnetic process by Osseweago, who was king of Atlantis, the sunken continent, about 6,4oo years ago’.
In 1893 she publishes an article in Light of Truth, vol XIII, No 8 (iapsop.com/archive/materials/light_of_truth/light_of_truth_v13_n8_aug_26_1893.pdf): this time, she seems to bypass Abbott as a teacher and goes straight to Osseweago: ‘(..) this process was taught to me by the grand Osseweago and my other spiritual guides’
In 1894 she publishes The Bridge Between Two Worlds, where she expands on the nature of the exercise, but no mention is given to Abbott nor the Spirits.
In 1898, in a letter to the Banner of Light on Magnetic Harmony (re-issued in A Happy Year, published 3 years before her death in a home fire (iapsop.com/ssoc/1899__judson___a_happy_year.pdf )) , her instructors are only referred as ‘my guides’ (She also mentions she started teaching the technique in 1890 by lessons at Clinton Camp)

Regarding the good doctor himself, the earliest reference I found was an entry in the San Francisco Golden Gate journal in 1886, where it is said: ‘Dr. H. W. Abbott, the magnetic healer, has removed his office to 1047 Mission street [San Francisco]. We hear some excellent reports of Dr. Abbott. He is doing a good work.’ He also advertised in the same number as:
‘H. W. Abbott, (late of Wichitaw, Kansas), NATURAL HEALER. Diagnoses Diseases without one word of information. Nearly all diseases that the human family are subject to, treated successfully. Terms Reasonable & Diagnosis Free.’ (golden_gate_v2_n23_jun_26_1886.pdf (iapsop.com))
He seems to have been touring: in 1888, in the Santa Barbara’s Morning Press, he’s referred as ‘the boy wonder of Kansas and Illinois’ in a brief notice updating the date of his local lecture. (Morning Press 28 April 1888 — California Digital Newspaper Collection (ucr.edu))
In 1889 the Golden Gate informs the ‘well known healer and test medium’ is back in San Francisco after a year and a half absence, where he is ‘holding developing and test circles’ with good success ( golden_gate_v8_n21_jun_8_1889.pdf (iapsop.com))
One year later he seems to be travelling again, he advertises in The Colorado Daily Chieftain as:
‘CLAIRVOYANT. Dr. H. W. Abbott, metaphysical healer and clairvoyant; disease diagnosed without asking questions; occult science taught; sittings daily from 9 am to 8 pm. Room 6, Baxter block.’ (The Colorado Daily Chieftain January 4, 1890 — Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection)
And in 1891 we find out the good doctor might not be as good after all, according to a Kansas paper: ‘News come from San Francisco that the relatives of Mrs. G. C. Williams, a widow worth $750,000, are fearful that she has been hypnotized into a marriage contract with Dr. H. W. Abbott, a spiritualist.’ (Macksville Independent from Macksville, Kansas on December 17, 1891 · 2 (newspapers.com)) (Interestingly, after 1891 Judson drops Abbott’s name when talking about her teachers)
Next mention I could find was in Los Angeles Herald, 1898, where it says: ‘Dr. H. W. Abbott, late of Chicago, will deliver an inspirational lecture on “The Power of Harmony” at Truth-seekers hall, 125 ½ S. Spring, followed by spirit messages through the mediumship of the lecturer and other mediums.’ (Los Angeles Herald 2 January 1898 — California Digital Newspaper Collection (ucr.edu))
It also appears that Dr Abbott had a side business as an inventor and seller of a sort of refrigeration system! according to two entries in the San Francisco Call (San Francisco Call 4 March 1900 — California Digital Newspaper Collection (ucr.edu)) and the Hanford Journal (Hanford Journal (Daily) 15 March 1900 — California Digital Newspaper Collection (ucr.edu)), both in 1900.
And that’s as much as I could gather, not sure how useful this could be but I enjoyed the detective work nonetheless!

Many thanks,
Manuel

replies.title: Re: Continuation from last week...
replies.content:
The Ring-Cosmos is you. Start from that point -- it's crucial. The Ring-Cosmos of any system is the system itself. The Ring-Chaos is the environment in which the system exists. Your parents' behavior toward you -- not their behavior overall, but the way they behaved toward you once you were born and became a factor in their lives -- was your original Ring-Chaos, which you used as a thrust block to define your original Ring-Pass-Not. comments.title: Quaker meditations
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Following on from the post about discursive meditation on the other blog last week, I was wondering if Quaker gatherings would qualify as discursive meditation?

If so, in my city their gatherings last one hour, which is longer than the maximum time you recommended for advanced meditators. (I am a beginner.)

I would like you opinion on the safety of joining such gatherings.

replies.title: Re: Quaker meditations
replies.content:
My wife is a Quaker and I enjoy attending their meetings from time to time. I'm interested to hear JMG's reply, but I don't think a Quaker meeting is a form of discursive meditation. It's a silent communal invocation ritual designed to call the Holy Spirit (or the "Light", in a more ecumenical setting) into the group. Sometimes it speaks through one or more of the participants, and sometimes it doesn't, but done properly it's not really an hour-long meditation on a specific phrase or theme. In fact, it's generally considered bad form to reply directly to someone else's comment - it's not nearly so "rational" a though process as what you get in a discursive meditation, but a more meandering path through an open, attentive, listening mind and a thoughtful, critically-thinking one.

I think you'll find it quite different from discursive meditation, but use your best judgment. You're opening your mind up in a group of different people who may be reaching out to a different deity than the one you have in mind, so "by their fruits ye shall know them." The two Quaker groups I have regularly attended meetings with were both great for my spiritual development, but my wife attended a third once that she described as in a perpetual conflict between a more liberal and more conservative faction who were continually battling it out in their comments rather than really listening carefully to anyone besides themselves.

replies.title: Re: Quaker meditations
replies.content: I've never attended a Quaker meeting, but (a) as far as I know Quakers don't generally practice discursive meditation, and (b) Quakers, unlike attendees at mindfulness meditation seminars, don't have a high psychiatric casualty rate. Those being the case, I'm pretty sure you'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: Quaker meditations
replies.content: Thank you for the replies.

replies.title: Re: Quaker meditations
replies.content:
I've participated in Quaker meetings, as well. I agree with the other commenters.

There have been splits between groups of Quakers. All agree that the divine light can be known within, and sometimes stirs one to be moved to speak up. Some have the entire meeting for worship being silent, except when participants feel moved to say something. Others have a meeting that is more similar to a routine Christian church service, with a few minutes for silent worship.

All Quakers would be comfortable identifying the inner light with the "still, small voice" and the inner knowing described by Jesus. One source of dispute is how much these themes are appropriate to take in an ecumenical sense, connecting with other world religions in an extra-Christian sense, or whether Quaker theology should stay within its Christian origins.

Meetings for worship are for each person to speak up if so moved. If the same Spirit is stirring up several to speak, then a consensus can be easily found. In general, one is discouraged from responding to or debating what others said, rather than speaking from one's own innate experience of inspiration.

At "meetings for worship and business," there is a more formal process to respond to what others said, and issues can be referred to committees. If the clerk, after asking the congregation, feels that all hearts are clear about a matter, the clerk can make a minute that is legally binding for the congregation. This generally replaces a voting process in most Quaker meetings.

- Mr. New-Writer

replies.title: Re: Health geomancy
replies.content:
Okay, this is a great example of the most common problem with reading house charts: getting bollixed up over the houses. If you're asking a health question it is ALWAYS the 6th house -- your 6th if you're asking for yourself or for a querent, the 6th from the relevant house if you or the querent are asking on behalf of someone else. If you're asking it on behalf of your daughter, you rotate the chart and take the 6th house from the 5th, or the 10th house.

Cast another chart, and use the 1st to represent you, the 5th to represent your daughter, and the 10th to represent her health. That will tell you if you or your daughter are doing something to cause it.

replies.title: Re: Podcast
replies.content:
It was Myke Hideous, not Michael Decon, who referred to himself as a Satanist. I have no idea what he considers Satan to be -- we didn't discuss it, so you'd have to ask him! As for Satanists in general, well, as you'd expect, no two of them seem to have exactly the same notion, and many of them don't worship anything at all.

When I say I don't believe in Satan, by the way, what I mean is that I don't believe that there's a single supreme power of evil in the cosmos. "Evil" is a very complex concept; when it doesn't simply mean "what I don't like" or "what I wish I had the guts to do" -- and very, very often that's all it means -- it works out to destructive imbalance. comments.title: Depression
comments.content:
Hello JMG, and thank you for your gracious use of time and effort.

I have a lot of mental illnesses, and I've found that meditation, banishing, and a sense of connection to the spiritual is a great boon for most of them. There is however, one exception; depression. No matter what I do, it seems as though I just lose the ability to do or feel anything constructive. For everything else, a judicious application of willpower is helpful and empowering, but with this, it feels like my will is lost in a hole and I can't find it.

I've muddled by without horrible mainstream medication for about a decade now, and I really don't want to chemicalize myself like that. To be clear, I am NOT asking for medical advice, alternative or otherwise. I would, however, like to know if there are any anti-depressive practices of an occult or magical nature that I could try before prostrating myself before the altar of Big Pharma. Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: Depression
replies.content:
Okay, this is what I was taught about depression.

Depression is one of the most challenging states to deal with because it's a tertiary emotional state. What that means is that it's layered over two other emotional states that are frozen in place, usually because of childhood experiences.

What happens is that as a child, you felt a primary emotion -- grief, fear, and shame are the usual primary emotions, and you can also have a blend of these -- which you couldn't deal with. In a healthy family a child who feels one of these can run to a parent and be comforted, and all's well -- but if the parent is the one causing the primary emotion, not just once but repeatedly, that can't happen, and the emotion becomes frozen into place. A pervasive state of grief, fear, or shame, is very difficult for a child to deal with, and so many children turn to anger as a secondary emotion. and feel that instead.

Anger is easier to deal with, because it's life-affirming and self-affirming -- you feel angry because of something that was done to you, and by doing so, you're affirming that you matter, that the thing that was done to you was wrong and worthy of anger. The problem is that in most unhealthy families, the child doesn't dare express anger toward its parents, or even let itself feel the anger. So depression comes to the rescue. The child learns to numb itself to its own emotional states.

That's very often necessary for the child's psychological survival, and sometimes -- if the parent is violently abusive -- to the child's physical survival. Fast forward a couple of decades, though, and you've got an adult who has trouble with depression. The anger and the primary emotion are still there, far down below the surface, and until the adult gets in touch with those feelings, experiences them, accepts them, and releases them, the depression will keep recurring because the deeper emotions are still there.

Journaling is a good way to work with that. The Order of Spiritual Alchemy, one of the traditions in which I've been initiated, has a specific system of journaling that it uses to help people with that, but plain ordinary journaling will also do the trick. Write about what upsets you, what depresses you, what hurts you. Get into the habit of getting your feelings down on paper, uncensored, uncondemned, and bit by bit it'll all come up into consciousness again. Pleasant? No, a lot of the times it hurts like anything, but it's a healing pain. That's certainly my experience.

replies.content: It's not currently active, but I inherited certain rights to it when my teacher John Gilbert died. I'm currently trying to figure out how best to pass on all the various traditions I received from him; I'll post something once I get that sorted out.

replies.title: Sycamores
replies.content:
The American Sycamore's latin name is Platanus Occidentalis. It is closely related to the "Plane" trees (also of the species Platanus)of Europe and Asia, such as the hybrid London Plane tree. I just did a search for "magical lore of plane trees," and found info that you might find interesting.

For trees of North America in general, you might check out Donna Beresford Kroeger. She is a fascinating woman raised by Irish elders. She now lives in Canada. She has wonderful books about Celtic lore of trees and she does a good job of interpreting North American trees through that lens. She is a scholar, scientist, and practitioner of old Celtic wisdom. She has a several wonderful books, my favorite is Arboretum Borealis. There is also a Native American woman, Robin Wall Kimmerer, whose wonderful book "Braiding Sweet Grass" covers spiritual and practical aspects of trees and plants. Both of these authors are in my pantheon of contemporary environmental writers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Traditional occult lore has it that one of the things that human beings are doing, as we struggle with the challenges of our own lives and our spiritual development, is making it possible for demonic beings to begin to glimpse the way to their own salvation. In Cabalistic terms, our Malkuth -- the lowest, most material realm of being on our plane -- is their Kether -- the highest, most spiritual realm of being on their plane.

As for gods, a lot of different kinds of beings get that label -- in a real sense, the word "god" means "something that humans worship" -- and that covers a lot of territory! In occult theory, however, most of the beings we worship were once at our stage of evolution, and some are not that far above us; they can certainly learn better.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't recommend reading him until you've got enough experience and knowledge to recognize where and why he went so disastrously wrong. He was certainly not a success -- to repeat a summary I've used before, if you start out rich, handsome, well educated, talented, and charismatic, and you end up as a burnt-out heroin addict in a small town nursing home with an estate worth fourteen shillings and a name you've personally made a laughing stock on three continents, you're doing something wrong.

On the other hand, if you know your way around astrology, cast his natal chart sometime. It's the chart of a bland little man, the sort of person you'd expect to work all his life in a minor bureaucracy and publish off-color poems in the kind of magazine that pays in copies. Crowley unquestionably transcended that destiny! That shows, if nothing else, that magic can give you a much more interesting life; if he'd done it right, it might also have helped him overcome his overinflated ego and his other personal problems -- but unfortunately that was not to be.

I have been chanting this mantra for many years so it is not a new thing to throw on. Then finish up the ritual as given.

Wow!! The ritual is balancing (literally, after this one-leg balancing yoga postures are rock solid), smoothly energizing for hours, and makes my etheric body pretty much bullet proof! Now no matter what sort of toxic narcissist I encounter (they are out there, in the streets and in the home!) my feathers remain serenely unruffled. I can’t encourage anyone enough who tries this ritual to put a heartfelt little prayer in step #3...it packs a lot of power. Thank you JMG for sharing this!

2. Last week someone asked if kombucha would have the same effect as regular vinegar for clearing up the etheric plane. Currently I have 10 gallons of kombucha brewing on my kitchen counter.....and have had at least that much for 5 years or so (small enterprise for sale and trade). Yes, my kitchen seems energetically fresh as a daisy :) Once I was away and my 14 year old daughter contacted me to say there was a demon floating around her room that was causing much distress to her and the dachshund. This kid means business when she makes a statement like that! I reminded her there was a bottle of kombucha vinegar (steeped with chamomile) under her bathroom sink that is used for any manner of skincare.....told her to get that out, uncap it, swirl it around and sit it on her bedside table. She reported back in a half an hour that the demon had departed and the dachshund was able to sleep. So acetic acid at 2% works fine......and kombucha is alive and seems to have very positive vibes.

3. On the last open post, some commenters we’re talking about dental health and oral hygiene. Don’t forget about the Ayurvedic regime of “oil pulling” 👍 ....been doing that for years to good effect. There is loads of info on the internet about it.....but you just have a spoonful of cold pressed oil (any kind will do but coconut is the most popular) first thing in the morning on an empty stomach, swish it around your mouth for 10 to 15 minutes (make your bed, run through the shower, recite your affirmation, etc) then spit it out (flush it! don’t put it down the sink drain), swish with salt water, then fresh water.....then take your cell salts ;) , brush your teeth and go on about your business. The oil mixed with saliva gets super sticky and all manner of bad bacteria and other nasty substances are “pulled” out into the oil and gotten rid of. It makes your mouth super clean! and can even get rid of horrible tastes from medications if you need assistance in that direction ...and it might make a good will exercise too!

Thanks for indulging my rambling 🤓 SundaraYogaShala

comments.title: First Party vs Third Party Geomancy
comments.content:
Someone has recently asserted to me that when reading a Geomantic chart for someone else, unless the person you are reading for casts the Geomantic figures themselves, the chart must be read as a third party question. As it happens, I had been wondering along these lines myself. I have not yet happened to do very many readings for others, so it's not something I've yet had opportunity to test much.

To take the practical example of Lady Cutekitten's lost $65 debit card. Last week I cast an initial question asking whether she might find it within a reasonable time frame.

[IMAGE:0]
(Mothers Conjunctio, Via, Laetitia, Populus)

The answer perfected to a fairly strong Yes via translation when read as 1st person. However, it seems that, treated as a 3rd party question, it is a weak "maybe" via sextile between F. Minor in turned Querent's 12th and turned Quesited 2nd. (Incidentally, Lady Cutekitten, the court of [Right Witness Tristitia / Left Witness Albus / Judge Fortuna Major] suggests that blessings may be in store but they'll take a while and may feel unpleasant, and will serve as a learning opportunity.)

For completion's sake, here is the followup chart-- I'll share it here. I asked, "Where can Lady Cutekitten find her lost $65 debit card?", treating it as a 2nd House question in which the figure in the 2nd, or whatever house it jumps to, shows where it may turn up.

[IMAGE:1]
(Mothers Via, Caput Draconis, Cauda Draconis, Cauda Draconis)

I treated this as a first party question last week, and since Caput Draconis traditional says that an object has not been lost at all, this is what I reported to her. It actually doesn't change too much if a third party question: then the figure in Lady Cutekitten's turned 1st (my 7th) remains Via, but the figure in her 2nd becomes Fortuna Minor, which means that the object "has only been overlooked and will turn up promptly if the querent looks for it more carefully".

Lady Cutekitten, the only other things I can glean from this if reading as 3rd person instead of 1st person are that: (1) since the turned 12th house is the same as the turned Querent in both charts, it is likely that the "hidden enemy" in this case is you, yourself; (2) in the 2nd chart, the turned Querent's character also passes to the turned 7th, perhaps involving a partner or counterpart that you live or work with closely; (3) the figure in question here representing both you and your "partner", and also sitting in the place of the Judge, is Via, which means travel, or a complete change in viewpoint; and (4) the Left Witness, which represents future and external influences in the matter, is Caput Draconis (pregnant potential) and it also appears in the 8th house of spirits-- suggesting to me that maybe you shouldn't count St Anthony out of the picture just yet here.

(1) JMG, I am aware that you don't specifically address the issue of whether it is important for a Querent to cast the figures themselves in "Art and Practice". I also know that you wrote that book some time ago, and I don't know how much reading for others you have done. Do you have any opinions or guidance to offer on the matter?

I will join to see if my interest in divination with the I Ching is useful.

Question: Where is Lady Cutekitten's lost $65 card?

The present is represented by hexagram 46, "Pushing Upward," with lines 1,3,and 4 changing. Yang (bright, masculine, active side of life) is increasing, changing the situation into the future represented by hexagram 54, "The Marrying Maiden."

Pushing Upward is a very positive indication of success, like a plant growing out of the ground. "A journey to the south brings good fortune." The South represents activity as opposed to retiring, resting, staying within at home.
Line 1 shows in general that pushing upwards is fortunate and successful.
Line 3 shows that like a military campaign which finds no adversary to oppose them, pushing upwards is like arriving at an empty city.
Line 4 shows that a king awarded someone Mount Ch'i, which was a fortunate situation, free of blame. (The I Ching is often concerned about whether or not there is a need to blame someone about the situation.) Mt. Ch'i was the place where a certain new king reverenced his ancestors, so in this case, the king is telling the ancestors or family gods that the rewarded person should be included in their favor.

Overall, Pushing Upward implies of something that "it will soon come up!"

"THE MARRYING MAIDEN, shows a young girl under the guidance of an older man who marries her." In this description, she should defer to his wise judgment. If one attempts to take the initiative, it will not work out well. Temporary situations need to be understood in the perspective of eternity.

What is most apparent, above and in front, is that the receptiveness of Earth is giving way to the movement and action of Thunder.
What is least apparent, below and behind, is that the following nature of Wind (ungrounded air) is giving way to the focused joy, pleasure, and attraction of Lake (grounded water, the droplets happy to be together).

Given this, I would suggest looking for the card
* To the south, especially any area where there is gardening or new spring growth poking through the soil
* To a place that one expects to find full but is now empty
* Related to ancestral traditions or to one's own spirituality
* Involving an older man, husband, or someone connected to husband's side of the family; has Lord Cutekitten been invited to join the search party?
* Where something windborne could arrive at a place where water accumulates (a sink could be represented by this, not just an outdoor lake)

- Mr. New-Writer

comments.title: Breathwork
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks in advance for any light you can shed. What’s your take on the various forms of breathwork? I’ve had lots of exposure to Holotropic Breathwork, Rebirthing, Wim Hof‘s work, and a couple others. I find them to be energizing and relaxing, and helpful in bringing current emotional or psychological issues into awareness.

I personally have not seen any downside to any of these practices but it occurs to me that altering one’s consciousness, even with what amounts to intentional hyperventilation, might result in unwanted energies coming to visit or in some other kind of longer-term imbalance. After all, Stan Grof developed Holotropic Breathwork as a safe and legal alternative to psychedelics, and I have read your advice that magical work and psychedelics do not mix well.

replies.title: Re: Breathwork

replies.title: Re: Breathwork
replies.content:
I don't have an enormous amount of direct experience with these particular methods, but breathwork in the sense of working with the breath was at the center of my spiritual path for several years. The breath is, in my mind, one of the most flexible tools available to an aspiring magician/spiritual practitioner. It is a neither small nor subtle thing that the words for spirit and psyche are connected to words for breathing in most languages. The three techniques you listed have always seemed to me like they were part of the same general milieu and would expect them to play well together.

Rebirthing : As far as I can tell, this a fairly good practice built on top of dubious theory. I dropped it after a few weeks of practice because, to me, it really felt like a weaker version of dissolving practices I already made use of, with a heavy dose of either delusion/materialism. As far as I could discern, the technique is all about trying to release trapped patterns in the etheric body, which it is capable of, but Orr's insistence that it is the result of childhood seemed incomplete and the description of being reborn as needlessly odd. Greer's discussions of initiation are, to my mind, a far more lucid and comprehensive model of what you are trying to do.

Wim Hof : Simple and powerful, as well as shockingly resilient even in the face of a lack of context. If I were only allowed to put three minutes a day into spiritual discipline, this is probably the exercise I would pick. The genius of the exercise is that in addition to involving the etheric body, is that the shifts in breathing pattern and constant drive towards fully opening up the awareness of the body as the breath passes through allows it to engage the astral as well. The downside, to such a powerful and concentrated technique is that it seems to be the spiritual equivalent of gasoline and can play really badly with certain other disciplines. It is worth noting that exercise or a difficult physical discipline is recommended after the breathing portion, I believe this to be a needed attempt at grounding; a vitally important and easily overlooked step. Trying to meditate or perform a ritual in the aftermath seems to result in mild hallucinations and the associated images to become inappropriately charged and can be difficult to handle.

Holotropic Breathwork : I actually only tried this technique twice before dropping it and have an intensely negative view of it. To my mind, it is EXACTLY what it was created to be, a legal substitute for drugs. It is worth noting that a couple of alternative-healthcare communities have issued warnings about it. My first started badly since it felt like the technique was somehow connected to something nasty. A few minutes in I stopped since my intuition was going haywire. Second time I tried with some more thorough preparations. Purification, banishment, systematic relaxation, then making careful connections to my body so I would have a better grasp of what was going on. Shortly after I started, it really felt like I was getting attention from a decidedly unpleasant egregore and the physical effects were not pleasing: the amygdala felt like it was temporarily shorted out in order to boost the pineal gland (which makes me think the dizziness may not be entirely the product of hyperventilation), the thymus felt like its activity was compromised, the diaphragm and intercostals felt like they developed a mechanical quality that helped maintain the heavy breathing but came at the price of the ability to breathe in a truly natural fashion for a while afterwards, and it made the etheric body feel sticky and like it was prone to just absorbing any influence around it, while the astral body was completely overstimulated and hyperactive, resulting in the anticipated hallucinations. Personally, I would stay far away from this technique and warn others against it.

Speaking generally about breathwork, the deeper you go, the more context and intent begin to shape the result of these sorts of practices. I personally think that it is always best to have some kind of system or framework for dealing with such things.

replies.title: Re: Breathwork
replies.content:
"I personally have not seen any downside to any of these practices"

The danger is that if the person guiding or leading the session is not equipped to stay present, alert, a neutral compassionate witness, then when the client has a re-experiencing of difficult issues or traumatic events, some kind of abreaction, this could result in the therapist/provider freaking out. Or, jumping in with unhelpful intellectual explanations that interrupt the client's own insightful discoveries. Or perhaps shove the client out the door "because our hour is up now" while the client is still in a fragile state, not yet emotionally and psychologically re-integrated to face current everyday life. Grof, Janov, Levine all discuss this issue.

- Mr. New-Writer

comments.title: How to know for sure
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Long time reader here, thanks for the provision if this great public good.

I guess ill start with some background. I moved to a new country to pursue some research/career related goals, but as I was leaving, my father got suddenly sick and passed away.Great man and his loss shook me for some time, and being away from home and alone made it harder. In these hard times I relied on a single friend here in this new place (female early 30s). We knew each other well from a few years back- we met in a studying/friendly environment some years earlier. I have always been quite friendly and outgoing, she always struck me as someone with depressive tendencies and some self steem issues but I didn't particularly thought it was too much or anything bad and was always OK with giving a pep talk. Anyway, after my fathers passing away we became closer, but I started seeing that in this case feeling sad, suffering and related emotions seemed to be a way of my friend to bond - and that has certainly not been my approach on life. If I was griefing at some given moment it was a out of a natural response to the first brush up with death, and the closeness of my father, and also in a way the mourning of a very happy period of my life in general. Anyway.. I soon approached again life with positivity and thankfulness for even having had my parent on the way.. but the problem is that I know believe that my friend is somewhat of an emotional vampire. I can see patterns of subtle manipulation, I can see how she employs the victim approach and has used it remarkably effectively to get to places. I have been studying some Hermetic magic/astrology/qabalah .. recently my gut feeling becomes extremely strong warning sign when I feel she is trying to bring me down or use some of the previous information I shared with her as a friend under my time of grief. I have a great appreciation for the past support .. but I think my sense of friendship and "loyalty" is a handicap here. How can I protect myself of this ? And how can I be sure that my gut feeling is right - is there a way to test this?

comments.title: Swimming in cold water
comments.content:
Would a swim in a pool of cold, swimming pool water have the same effects as a cold shower? Bardon mentions that you should dump the cool water you bathe in or at least make sure another human doesn’t touch it.

I already take cold showers although it doesn’t get too cold here in the tropics.

I’m wondering if the sunlight would clear out other people’s etheric residue in the swimming pool water or not.

replies.title: Re: Swimming in cold water
replies.content: Unfortunately not. The water in a swimming pool will have a lot of other people's etheric energy in it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG, thanks in advance for the answer.

I was wondering if one has to cross the abyss before developing a mental body, or is it possible to develop a mental body and cross the abyss after one is done with physical incarnations?

For some reason my subconscious spat an answer to this question at me in hypnopompia with a few reasons for it (including a related past MM answer), and I don't know if its accurate. I can meditate on it more/let it stew if you think this is that kind of question. Its hard to discern if some questions are "meditate on it" questions or not.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: As I understand it, you can't actually cross the abyss while in material incarnation -- you can at most get a glimpse of what's on the other side, and come into contact with your higher and divine genius, which exists on the other side. Transforming your mental sheath into a mental body is the passing of the Veil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Why can't you turn your mental sheath into a mental body while in material incarnation?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Please reread what I wrote. You can turn your mental sheath into a mental body while incarnate. You can't cross the abyss while incarnate. These two are not the same thing.

comments.title: The 5 elements and meditation
comments.content:
Hi!

A few days back I was wondering why the tetrahedron is associated with the element of fire and air with the octahedron and so on. So I decided to meditate on the above.

It was astonishingly difficult to get anywhere with those meditations, except for the cube and the earth.

Presumably you have done so before. Any hints on the above.

Many thanks

replies.title: Re: The 5 elements and meditation
replies.content: Most people need to start geometrical meditations at a much simpler stage -- the point, the line, the circle, and then build up from there. I also encourage a close reading of a book or two on sacred geometry; Miranda Lundy's fine little book Sacred Geometry is a good one to start with.

comments.title: UFO and the abductees phenomenon
comments.content:
Hello!

What is your understanding of the abductees phenomenon and UFOs'? Are there any links with the occult lore that refers to succubus?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: UFO and the abductees phenomenon
replies.content: Okay, good -- you're paying attention. The book you want to read, if you haven't already, is Passport to Magonia by Jacques Vallee; it shows that one aspect of today's UFO phenomenon is indistinguishable from medieval fairy lore, right down to the abductees and the fixation on human reproduction. Yes, there are links to the medieval lore of incubi and succubi as well.

comments.title: Culpepper's Complete Herbal
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

Apologies if this is received too late. Is there a particular edition of Nicholas Culpepper's Complete Herbal that you would recommend to the novice of herbalism and natural magic? Thank you in advance.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Culpepper's Complete Herbal
replies.content:
You're 17 minutes ahead of the closing bell, so you're fine. I'm not familiar with the various editions out there; the one I use is Culpeper's Complete Herbal and English Physician Enlarged (Meyerbooks, 1990).

While you're at it, if you can, you might want to pick up Graeme Tobyn's fine book Culpeper's Medicine: A Practice of Western Holistic Medicine, which is a very good modern introduction to Culpeper.

replies.title: Re: Culpepper's Complete Herbal
replies.content:
YOU might also like
A Modern Herbal (Volume 1, A-H): The Medicinal, Culinary, Cosmetic and Economic Properties, Cultivation and Folk-Lore of Herbs, Grasses, Fungi, Shrubs & Trees with Their Modern Scientific Uses Paperback – June 1, 1971

and there is a second volume also.

comments.content: Are tantric sexual practices as dangerous as kundalini yoga? Or is it something that is reasonably safe to try?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on which tantric practices you have in mind. Some are as safe as any other form of sexual interaction, some do things with energy centers and energy channels that can be risky, though I don't know of anything as risky as kundalini yoga! As with all Asian spiritual practices, finding a competent teacher is a must.

comments.title: Moons and beings
comments.content:
Late Greetings JMG!
Long time lurker, first time poster. (At least on this site - I've enjoyed your work for a while.)

1. A friend asked about full moon practices and I sent this. (Please correct or extend if appropriate):

Best practices for moon work (gathered from a few different sources over the years):

The waxing of the moon (right now before it is full) is for growing and creation, and whatever you are cultivating should be put firmly into place with intention by the moment of the full moon (11:30ish tonight.)

After the full moon, the moon is waning until the new moon, this is primarily a time for resting and contemplation and maybe some light preparation but not generally for putting things into action. Some say it's also a time for undoing - but "undoing" magic is not generally very effective, because the act puts more energy into whatever you are trying to undo. Better to just let go.

In short - it is always more effective to build up what you want than to work against what you don't want. (i.e. don't try to quit things, instead start doing things to replace what you want to stop.) So, think of the changes you seek and the things and relationships you want, and while the moon is waxing (getting fuller) put energy into those things. Do something at the full moon to solidify or acknowledge that work; then let go and rest until the new moon.

For example: After a new moon, start to make changes and create routines to build up what you want, then do something to celebrate those changes on the full moon, and after the full moon just keep following those routines without big changes and let yourself rest and build energy for the next round.

2. A question(s) of deeper import to me:
Backstory: I have been dabbling in various forms of magical living but for the most part have only fully incorporated natural magic(s) into my life, things that others might call superstitions. My family and I forage, garden biodynamically, and use herbal medicines and homeopathy. I have practices for ancestor connection and for acknowledging and showing respect to land and house spirits. I have started and stopped multiple ritual practices starting with some Wiccan/new-age stuff but quickly moving into Druidic practices (with your books being significant influences on my thoughts and practice - thank you for your teachings btw!) I spend time each day observing nature, discoursively meditate, and do daily readings (all of which I've started and stopped at points over the last 6 years and am now restarting.)

Here's the rub: I have tried the sphere of protection with various deities over the years and get hung up on that part. I know they can be substituted for other meaningful directional/elemental correlatives and yet I firmly experience a heavily populated world (on multiple levels) and wish to participate in that ecosystem and build relationships. My hangup started when I realized that people I knew who were "light and love" neopagans received "channeled" info and sometimes had incredible experiences but yet didn't necessarily spread light and love outward... and sometimes were actually a nexus of others suffering. I had a clear feeling that they were connecting to other beings but I had a gut feeling that those beings were just as jealous and capricious as we corporeal beings are. At this point I pulled back from my own ritual practices to reassess. So I've built up other practices. I've found myself at various points serendipitously presented with members of the Norse pantheon (yet they are intimidating, and frankly, just as Christianity is tainted for me by Christians I know, so it is with Norse paganism.)

Ok, so question: you recommend at times being very cautious with other realms and ritual work; yet, in the fundamental practice for ritual work (one which is intended to protect and balance) you suggest invoking deities, which feels like an advanced undertaking to me; is there something about this practice that is doing something different other than actually calling upon and/or connecting with the deities? For example, is speaking the deity names more of a resonance or entrainment than an actual call or connection...?

Thank you. I feel joy and relief at being able to ask someone I respect in these matters. I feel appreciation for what you do for this community.

replies.title: Re: Moons and beings
replies.content:
1) This seems more or less accurate to me

2) Calling on deities isn't an advanced practice; three-year-olds do it all the time, once they've been taught a basic prayer. On the other hand, if you aren't comfortable invoking deities, don't. Calling on a deity is indeed a request for connection and contact with an actual being; it's something that humans have been doing and deities have been responding to for a very long time, but if it's something that makes you uneasy, don't worry about it.

replies.title: Re: Moons and beings
replies.content: Thank you for the last minute answers; it opens new trains of thought to meditate along.
